title: Araghchi Reveals Turkey Thwarted Kurdish Offensive Against Iran, Cyprus Marks 52nd Anniversary Amid Peace Push
subhead: Iran's foreign minister confirmed Turkey played an 'effective' role in blocking a US-Israeli-backed Kurdish ground offensive from Iraqi Kurdistan, the first official confirmation from Tehran. Meanwhile, Cyprus marked 52 years since Turkey's 1974 intervention as VP Yilmaz pushed a two-state framework and President Christodoulides signaled readiness for UN-backed talks. The US-Iran war entered its tenth consecutive day with 17 American fatalities, mediators proposed a 10-day ceasefire, and Houthis declared a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia.

sections.heading: Iran Confirms Turkey Blocked Kurdish Offensive From Iraqi Kurdistan
sections.body:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed in a lengthy interview with Iranian media on Sunday that Turkey played an 'effective' role in thwarting a US- and Israeli-backed Kurdish insurgency that was to have been launched from Iraqi Kurdistan into Iran. Araghchi said he contacted Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammed Shia al-Sudani, Iraqi Kurdish leaders, and Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan early in the conflict after receiving reports that Trump had reached out to regional leaders about plans for the 'fragmentation of Iran.'

Araghchi said he warned them that 'if the slightest threat emerges toward us from the Iraqi Kurdistan region, we would confront it … and we would certainly respond.' Turkey, he added, 'talked to the Americans and adopted a very firm position in this regard. A series of efforts foiled this plot.' He did not elaborate further.

The revelation sheds new light on Trump's claim in late June that Erdogan had 'considered entering the war on Iran's side' but stayed out after Trump urged him to. Israeli public broadcaster Kan reported that Turkey had threatened to provide air support to Iran if Kurdish forces invaded as part of a Mossad-backed ground operation. Al-Monitor had previously reported on the CIA- and Mossad-backed plan to arm Kurdish groups for an incursion into Iran.
sections.whyItMatters: This is Tehran's first official confirmation of Ankara's direct role in blocking a Kurdish front against Iran. It confirms Turkey's dual calculus: preventing a PKK-linked entity from gaining power next door mattered more than aligning with the US-Israeli position on Iran. The disclosure also reframes Erdogan's diplomatic stance — he was not merely staying out of the war but actively shaping its outcome on a critical front.

sections.heading: Cyprus at 52: Yilmaz Pushes Two-State Frame, Christodoulides Awaits Turkey's Response
sections.body:
Monday marked the 52nd anniversary of Turkey's 1974 intervention in Cyprus, with competing narratives on full display. Turkish Vice President Cevdet Yilmaz attended ceremonies in north Nicosia, declaring that the 'peace operation brought peace and tranquillity not only to the Turkish Cypriot people, but also to the Greek Cypriots and to the island in its entirety.' He argued that 'approaches which reduce the Turkish Cypriots to the status of a minority' would fail, insisting that the path forward required accepting the 'sovereign equality and equal international status of the two states.'

Turkey's foreign ministry reinforced this position, citing the 1960 Treaty of Guarantee as the legal basis for the intervention and pledging to 'work tirelessly for the recognition of the Turkish Cypriots to sovereign equality and equal international status.' Israel, meanwhile, issued a statement reaffirming 'unwavering support for the sovereignty, independence, and territorial integrity of the Republic of Cyprus,' calling Turkey's continued military presence 'unacceptable.'

Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides said he is ready to attend an enlarged meeting on the Cyprus problem as soon as this summer, but stressed that 'everything depends on Turkey's reaction.' He confirmed that UN Secretary-General Guterres will visit Cyprus next week as part of an effort to revive stalled peace talks, and that EU envoy Raffaele Fitto may also visit. Guterres has been pursuing an initiative since March based on discussions with Erdogan.
sections.whyItMatters: The dueling messages — Yilmaz advocating two-state recognition, Christodoulides pushing for a bizonal federation — show how far apart the parties remain. But Guterres's upcoming visit and the EU's enhanced involvement create a diplomatic window that could narrow if Turkey's EU-accession calculus shifts in the current regional realignment.

sections.heading: US-Iran War Enters Tenth Day: Pezeshkian Declares Full-Scale War, Mediators Push 10-Day Ceasefire
sections.body:
The US-Iran conflict escalated dramatically over the weekend. Three US service members were killed — two in an Iranian strike on Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan, and one during a controlled detonation of an Iranian drone in Iraq's Kurdistan Region. That brings the total American fatalities in the conflict to 17. President Trump vowed Iran would 'pay many times over' for each death.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian declared that Iran is 'engaged in a full-scale war' with the United States. The US carried out its tenth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, targeting bridges and electrical infrastructure in southern Iran near the Strait of Hormuz. Iran retaliated by targeting US military assets in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain, and an alleged command center in Syria's al-Tanf.

Despite the escalation, diplomatic channels remain open. Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei confirmed that 'ideas have been conveyed to us by certain mediators' for de-escalation. Bloomberg reported that mediators have passed Iran a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire to revive an interim deal, according to a senior Iranian official who spoke to Reuters. Iran also said it disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz without permission, and a ship was reported burning near the Omani coastline in the strait.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict has entered a new intensity phase with declared full-scale war status from Iran and sustained US bombing. Yet both sides continue signaling openness to diplomatic off-ramps. Turkey's confirmed role in blocking the Kurdish front and its mediation positioning make Ankara a consequential actor in any ceasefire framework — which complicates its relationships with both Washington and Tehran.

sections.heading: Houthis Declare Maritime Embargo on Saudi Arabia, Threatening Red Sea Oil Route
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi rebels announced a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia on Monday, declaring it effective immediately under the principle of 'an eye for an eye.' Saudi Arabia condemned the announcement, calling it a deflection from Houthi mismanagement and public discontent.

The declaration is significant because Saudi Arabia has been sending roughly 4.5 million barrels per day of oil through the Red Sea, mainly to Asia, to bypass the Iranian-blockaded Strait of Hormuz. If enforced, a Houthi blockade would close off the last reliable alternative route for Gulf oil exports. Saudi Defense Minister Khalid bin Salman has suggested to counterparts that Riyadh has wide latitude from the Trump administration to conduct offensive strikes on the Houthis, though other Saudi officials say the kingdom's posture remains defensive.

The Houthis and Saudi Arabia traded fire last week for the first time in years, threatening the 2022 UN-backed truce. The trigger was an Iranian plane attempting to land in Sanaa, challenging the status quo of flights restricted to Cairo and Amman.
sections.whyItMatters: For Turkey, a Houthi blockade of the Red Sea compounds the energy disruption from the Hormuz crisis. Turkey imports most of its oil via seaborne routes. If both Hormuz and the Red Sea become unreliable, the Black Sea and overland routes gain even greater strategic importance — reinforcing the value of Turkey's energy transit position but also increasing import costs.

sections.heading: Russian Gas to Turkey Falls 4%, CPC Terminal Hit by Ukrainian Drones Again
sections.body:
Russia's pipeline gas supplies to Turkey fell 4% year-on-year in January-May 2026, reaching 9.6 billion cubic meters, according to TASS calculations based on Turkey's Energy Market Regulatory Authority (EPDK) data. In May alone, Turkey imported 1.27 bcm from Russia via TurkStream and Blue Stream.

The decline comes amid repeated attacks on Gazprom's export infrastructure. The most recent strike targeted the Krasnodarskaya compressor station of the Blue Stream pipeline on July 7 — coinciding with the NATO summit in Ankara. Despite the damage, supply disruptions were avoided. TASS also reported that Ukrainian drone attacks have again suspended loadings at the CPC Black Sea terminal near Novorossiysk. A drone struck the tanker Nelsa on July 20, the second attack in under 24 hours, forcing the Caspian Pipeline Consortium to halt crude exports for the second time in days.

The CPC pipeline carries roughly 1.5 million barrels per day of mainly Kazakh crude to global markets. The repeated strikes highlight the vulnerability of Black Sea energy infrastructure to the Ukraine-Russia conflict's spillover.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's energy supply chain is being squeezed from two directions: Ukrainian drones targeting Black Sea infrastructure near its sphere of influence, and the Iran-driven Hormuz disruption pushing up global oil prices. The 4% dip in Russian gas imports, while manageable, is notable given Turkey's heavy reliance on Russian supply. Combined with the CPC disruptions and Hormuz instability, Turkey faces a trifecta of energy route risks.

sections.heading: TBMM Defense Commission Wraps Up US Visit Amid Akar-Mast Controversy
sections.body:
Parliamentary Defense Commission Chairman and AK Party Kayseri MP Hulusi Akar pushed back forcefully against media coverage of the commission's official visit to the United States. Akar, Turkey's former defense minister, was portrayed by the opposition newspaper Sozcu as having made a personal visit to a disabled American veteran — identified as House Foreign Affairs Committee Chairman Brian Mast — rather than conducting official parliamentary diplomacy.

Akar clarified that the meetings were part of the TBMM National Defense Commission's official diplomatic program, involving commission chairs, members of Congress, and decision-makers. He emphasized that Turkey's national interests, counter-terrorism resolve, and regional positions were conveyed 'in the strongest possible way.' He noted the commission has been conducting a months-long review of veterans' and martyrs' rights, holding 26 meetings with 80 representatives from 25 NGOs and 120 from 20 public institutions, now in their final stage.

The visit comes at a sensitive moment in US-Turkey relations, with CAATSA sanctions relief and F-35 sales under discussion. Mast, a Florida Republican, is known for pro-Israel positions and for having expressed support for an independent Kurdistan — a position that directly conflicts with Turkish policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The Akar-Mast meeting, even if officially routine, is politically sensitive given Mast's past statements on Kurdish independence and the timing of CAATSA discussions. That Akar felt compelled to publicly clarify the visit's scope underscores how fraught any US-Turkey defense engagement remains in domestic politics.

sections.heading: Turkey Swelters Under 45-Degree Heatwave, Anantara Announces 2 Billion TL Cesme Investment
sections.body:
Southern Turkish provinces including Antalya and Adana recorded temperatures reaching 45 degrees Celsius as an extreme heatwave grips the country, according to local reports. The extreme heat is part of a broader pattern of record temperatures across the Mediterranean this summer.

On the economic side, Minor Hotels' luxury Anantara brand announced its first project in Turkey, a 2 billion TL investment in the Anantara Retreat Cesme within the Castello Fontana development. The 260,000-square-meter experiential tourism complex will include hotel villas with private pools and luxury suites. Arikan Project Development chair Orfea Tito Arik said the project, under development for five years, aims to 'carry Cesme to the position it deserves in global tourism.' Construction is slated to begin in October 2026, with the complex expected to host 1,500 guests upon completion.

The Central Bank of Turkey (TCMB) is set to release May international investment position data and its July market participant survey on Monday, key indicators for tracking capital flows and inflation expectations amid global energy price volatility. TurkStat will also release May agricultural input price indices and June domestic producer price indices.
sections.whyItMatters: The heatwave strains Turkey's agriculture and energy grids, while the luxury tourism investment signals continued foreign confidence in Turkey's Aegean coast. The TCMB data releases come at a critical moment — with Brent above $90 and the lira under pressure from global risk-off sentiment, the central bank's inflation and capital flow data will be closely watched.

bottomLine: Iran's public confirmation that Turkey actively prevented a Kurdish ground offensive from Iraqi Kurdistan is the day's most significant strategic revelation — it confirms Ankara prioritized its own Kurdish-security calculus over alignment with the US-Israeli campaign against Iran, and positions Turkey as a behind-the-scenes power broker in the conflict. The Cyprus anniversary and Guterres's upcoming visit add a diplomatic front to watch. The US-Iran war continued to escalate, with Pezeshkian declaring full-scale war and Houthis threatening to close off Saudi Arabia's Red Sea oil route, compounding the energy disruption already squeezing Turkey's import bill. Between Russian gas supply dips, CPC terminal disruptions, and Hormuz instability, Turkey's energy security calculus is being tested from every direction.
sources: Al-Monitor, Cyprus Mail, NPR, TASS, Middle East Eye, Haber7, BloombergHT, Anadolu Agency, Ekonomim, Greek City Times, Euronews, Reuters, Bloomberg, Malatya Guncel
